stem: In how many ways can a committee of 2 people be chosen from a group of 5 people?
In how many ways can a committee of 3 people be chosen from a group of 6 people?

quantityA: The number of ways to choose 2 people from a group of 5 (order doesn't matter)
quantityB: The number of ways to arrange 2 people from a group of 5 in a line (order matters)
solutionStandard: Combinations: \(C(5,2)=10\). Permutations: \(P(5,2)=20\). Since order-matters counting is always at least as large, Quantity B is greater.

stem: A standard deck has 52 cards. In how many ways can a hand of 2 cards be chosen (order doesn't matter)?
A pizza shop offers 6 toppings. How many different 3-topping pizzas can be made (no repeated toppings, order doesn't matter)?
A group has 4 women and 5 men. In how many ways can a team of 2 women and 2 men be chosen?
Which of the following scenarios are combination problems (order doesn't matter)? Select all that apply.

options.text: Choosing 3 books to bring on a trip from a shelf of 10
Awarding gold, silver, and bronze medals to 3 of 10 racers
Selecting a 5-person jury from a pool of 20
Assigning students to numbered seats in a row

solutionStandard: (A) and (C) are unordered selections — a combination. (B) assigns distinct roles (gold/silver/bronze) to specific people, so order/position matters — a permutation. (D) assigns distinct seat numbers, so it's also a permutation.

stem: In how many ways can a committee of at least 1 person be chosen from a group of 3 people?
A committee of 4 is chosen from 5 women and 4 men. In how many ways can the committee include exactly 2 women and 2 men?
solutionStandard: Choose 2 of 5 women: \(C(5,2)=10\). Choose 2 of 4 men: \(C(4,2)=6\). Total: \(10 \times 6 = 60\).

stem: How many diagonals does a hexagon (6 sides) have?
solutionStandard: The number of segments connecting any 2 of the 6 vertices is \(C(6,2)=15\). Subtract the 6 sides themselves (which connect adjacent vertices, not diagonals): \(15-6=9\).
